executive_summary: Sunday placed orders were soft but not alarming: 12 vs 12.9 avg (-7.0%), helped by a large $63.06 average order size ex. HST. The real issue was acquisition: $134.65 in ads produced 2 new customers, or $67.33 each vs the $25 ceiling.
analyst_brief:
Yesterday was not a demand-collapse day. It was a weak new-customer day hidden inside an otherwise decent Sunday.

The good news: 12 placed orders is only 7.0% below the 7-day average, and placed order value was $756.77 ex. HST, 7.0% above average. That happened because the average order size was strong at $63.06 ex. HST, safely above the $45 target and nearly 15% above the 7-day average. The day’s orders were mostly regulars: 8 of 12 came from customers on their third order or later. That is healthy proof that the service keeps working for people who have already tried it.

The problem is what paid spend bought. Google Ads spent $134.65 and the DB shows only 2 new customers. That is $67.33 per new customer, almost 2.7x the $25 ceiling. The 7-day cost per new customer is also high at $49.14, so this is not just one noisy Sunday. Do not soften this by spreading ad spend across all 12 orders: 10 of those orders were returning customers, and yesterday’s acquisition budget did not create them. The clean read is: paid ads brought in too few new customers for the money.

The delivery numbers look ugly but should be read as an operations timing signal, not a growth signal by themselves. Only 4 orders delivered, with $244.63 ex. HST revenue, both less than half the 7-day average. That deserves a check for whether Sunday had fewer scheduled deliveries or a route/supply constraint. It does not change the marketing conversion read, because placed orders are the demand number.

The highest-leverage move today is to tighten paid acquisition without touching negatives. The obvious place to inspect is the GTA Click campaign: it spent $56.22, more than 40% of yesterday’s spend, for 1 Google Ads registration, while the whole account only produced 2 DB new customers. Before changing bids or keywords, line up yesterday’s new-customer orders against campaign/city signals and ask: did GTA Click produce actual first orders, or mostly clicks and registrations? If the answer is mostly registrations, shift budget toward the city campaigns showing real order creation once that mapping is visible. Hamilton and Ottawa each reported 2 Google Ads registrations on modest spend; they are worth checking against DB first-order geography before moving money.

Organic search has a smaller but cleaner opportunity: Hamilton’s city page had 414 impressions, ranked around position 9.9, and got only 1 click. Because the city pages share template-level meta title and description, do not treat this as a one-page title tweak. The body content is the editable per-city lever. A 20-minute Hamilton body copy pass focused on pickup/delivery, wash and fold, and local intent is reasonable if paid-ad investigation is already in motion.

Service quality remains acceptable but watch WashPro 2323: 4 low ratings in 30 days at a 2.75 average is enough to merit operator review. Yesterday’s cancellation rate moved slightly down vs yesterday’s rolling reading (-0.1pp), and failed payments stayed at zero.

days.executive_summary: Wed placed orders at 10 (vs 10.9 avg, -8.3%) — regulars drove 80% of volume but only 1 new customer came in (vs 2.4 avg, -58%). Deliveries soft at 8 ($426.50 ex. HST, -23.4%). Blended cost per new customer healthy at $10.56.
days.analyst_brief:
**Wednesday was a regulars day.** Eight of 10 placed orders came from customers who've ordered three or more times. Only 1 new customer showed up — the lowest in the 7-day window and well below the 2.4 daily average. One second-order customer rounded it out.

That's not alarming on its own — single-day new customer counts bounce around a lot at this volume. But it's worth watching. Looking at the week, the daily new customer numbers have been: 3, 3, 2, 1, 5, 2, 1. Sunday (May 4) spiked to 5 with the highest spend day ($148.18), and Wednesday came in at just 1 on lower spend ($105.56). There's no clear trend yet — it's noisy — but two of the last three days landed at 1-2 new customers.

**The delivered side was soft but not unusual.** Eight deliveries for $426.50 ex. HST. That's -23.4% vs the 7d average, but the average is inflated by Monday's 16-delivery day ($884.88 ex. HST). Delivered AOV was solid at $53.31 ex. HST, above the 7d average.

**Cost per new customer is in excellent shape.** At $10.56 per placed order against $105.56 in ad spend, you're well under the $25 ceiling. The 7d average is $10.36 — consistent and healthy. Even if you count only the 1 new customer, you spent $105.56 to acquire them, which is high for a single day — but that's not the right way to read it, since regulars also come through paid search. The blended number is what matters and it's strong.

**Search terms worth noting.** The top three search terms by spend each cost $10-14 for a single click with no conversion: "laundry pick up and delivery near me" ($13.72), "laundry pickup and delivery service near me" ($11.02), and "pick up laundry service toronto" ($10.41). These are high-intent queries but the click costs are steep and none converted on Wednesday. This is a normal variance day, not necessarily a problem — but if the pattern repeats across several days, it'd be worth reviewing whether the landing pages match the search intent.

"Poplin laundry service" appeared ($3.09, 1 click, no conversion) — someone searching for a competitor and seeing Tumbil. These searches for other brands that still convert are valuable when they do convert; worth keeping an eye on this one.

**On the organic side**, "laundry service barrie" is sitting at position 11.3 with 31 impressions and 2 clicks — right on the edge of page 1. The Barrie city page is at position 8.3 with 157 impressions and 4 clicks. Barrie is pulling decent organic interest. The Google Ads campaign for Barrie spent $4.62 with 0 conversions — organic may be the better channel there.

**The 7-day cancellation rate is 2.6%, unchanged day-over-day and well below the 30-day baseline of 4.6%.** Zero failed payments. Service quality looks excellent — 14 ratings yesterday, all 5 stars, and only 1 low rating (a 3-star) in the entire 7-day window. The rating response rate of 93.4% is unusually high, which means you're getting a nearly complete picture of customer satisfaction.

**Reorder trends**: The most recent complete cohort (week of Apr 13) had 9 new customers and 4 reordered within 21 days — a 44.4% reorder rate. That's the second-best reading in the 8-week window, behind only the Feb 16 cohort (50%). Small sample, but the direction is positive.

**Bottom line for today**: Nothing is broken. The business ran on its regulars yesterday, which is what you want — 80% of orders from people who keep coming back. The worry would be if new customer acquisition stays at 1/day for multiple days running. Tomorrow's number will tell you if Wednesday was just noise or the start of a dip.

days.executive_summary: Thursday placed 10 orders ($611.27 ex. HST), roughly in line with the 7-day average. But only 1 new customer showed up — regulars carried the day (7 of 10). Cost per new customer hit $122.92, nearly 5x the $25 ceiling. Deliveries were soft at 7 ($348.88 ex. HST, -36.2% vs avg).
days.analyst_brief:
**The new-customer pipeline nearly shut off on Thursday.** Only 1 new customer placed an order, versus the 7-day average of 2.1. That single acquisition absorbed the full $122.92 of ad spend, pushing cost per new customer to nearly 5x the $25 ceiling. This is a noisy metric on low-volume days — the 7-day average is $55.73, which is already over the ceiling — but Thursday was especially stark.

The good news: the business didn't fall apart. Regulars placed 7 orders and 2 second-timers came back, so placed volume held at 10 (only 12% below average). Average order size was $61.13 ex. HST, the highest single day this week, pulled up by the regulars ordering bigger loads. Total placed order value was $611.27 ex. HST, nearly flat to the $627.19 average. The regulars are doing their job.

**Delivered orders were genuinely weak at 7** (vs 10.7 avg), and revenue followed at $348.88 ex. HST (vs $546.84 avg). That's a 36% miss. Deliveries are a lagging indicator — these are orders that were placed days ago — so Thursday's placed performance won't show up in delivery numbers for a bit. Still, the delivery dip has been building: Monday was 16, then 8, now 7. Worth watching whether this is just timing or something operational.

**On the ad side, one search term deserves a closer look.** "Simple laundry" cost $30.58 on a single click with no conversion. That's a competitor name eating a quarter of the day's spend in one shot. I'm flagging it for manual review — not recommending any keyword changes, just making sure you see it. The rest of the search terms were typical city and service queries. "Laundry service near me" drove 2 conversions across its appearances, which is solid.

**The 7-day cost per new customer at $55.73 is the more concerning trend.** It's been above the $25 ceiling consistently. The week acquired 15 new customers on $836 of total spend. That math ($55.73 per new customer) only works if the customers who come back bring the blended cost down — and the reorder data is encouraging there. The April 13 cohort (the most recent with full 21-day data) had a 45.5% reorder rate, the best since mid-February. So people who sign up are coming back at a healthy rate; the issue is the front of the pipeline, not what happens after.

**Cancellations continue improving.** The rolling 7-day rate dropped to 1.2% (down 1.4 percentage points vs yesterday's reading of the same metric), well below the 30-day baseline of 4.3%. Zero failed payments. Ratings remain excellent at 4.9 average, with 9 of 10 ratings at 5 stars and zero low ratings.

**On search visibility:** all 42 monitored pages are indexed, no flags. Ottawa's city page gets the most impressions of any city page (493) but only a 1% click-through rate — the page ranks around position 8.7, so it's visible but the listing isn't compelling enough to click. Hamilton is similar: 432 impressions, 0.93% click-through, position 8.1. Since the meta titles and descriptions are template-level across all 24 city pages, any improvement there would lift all of them. Mississauga (331 impressions, 0.3% click-through) would benefit the most from better meta copy on a per-impression basis.

**Bottom line:** Thursday was a regulars-carry-the-load day. The business is healthy operationally (great ratings, low cancellations, strong reorder rates). The weakness is at the top — not enough new people are finding Tumbil and converting, and when they do, the cost is too high. The "simple laundry" spend leak is worth investigating manually, and the city page meta descriptions are a low-effort opportunity to improve organic click-through across the board.

days.executive_summary: Friday placed orders dipped to 9 (vs 10.3 avg, -12.6%), carried almost entirely by regulars (8 of 9). Only 1 new customer acquired — cost per new customer spiked to $107.79, 4x the $25 ceiling. Deliveries were solid at 12 ($645.53 ex. HST, +12.2% vs avg).
days.analyst_brief:
**Friday was a regulars-only day.** Eight of nine orders came from people who've ordered three or more times. One new customer. Zero second-orders. That's the entire story.

The cost per new customer number — $107.79 — looks alarming, but it's mostly a small-denominator problem. You spent $107.79 on ads and got 1 new customer instead of the usual 2. On a per-order basis (blending new and returning), each order cost $11.98 in ad spend, which is actually below the 7-day average of $12.43. The ads drove traffic (62 clicks), but that traffic mostly didn't convert to *new* accounts.

This is the third day in a row with only 1 new customer. The 7-day average has drifted down to 1.9/day. For context, Sunday May 4 pulled in 5 new customers, which is propping up that average — strip that day out and the other six days averaged 1.3. The front of the pipeline is thinning.

**Where the ad money went.** "Laundry service toronto" ate $32.70 for 2 clicks and zero conversions. "Toronto wash and fold laundry service" cost $8.13 for 1 click, no conversion. These broad Toronto terms are expensive because they're competitive, and on days like Friday they can burn budget without producing a single new customer. The one search term that did convert — "wash and fold laundry service near me" — cost $2.44. Worth watching whether the high-intent "near me" queries consistently outperform the city-name queries in conversion.

**The good news: regulars are reliable and happy.** 8 orders from regulars, average order size of $58.00 ex. HST (above the $45 target), and a perfect 5.0 rating from 9 reviews. The 7-day cancellation rate is 1.4%, well below the 30-day baseline of 4.1%. The service itself is in great shape.

**The worry is the second-order gap.** Zero customers came back for their second order on Friday. The 7-day average is 1.3/day, but it's been 0 twice this week (Friday and Saturday). This is where customers either become regulars or disappear. The 21-day reorder data shows the most recent measurable cohort (week of Apr 13) came back at 40%, which is solid. But the daily second-order count is worth watching — if it stays at 0-1 for another week, that 40% cohort rate isn't translating to volume because there aren't enough new customers feeding in.

**On organic search:** a few city pages are getting seen by Google but not clicked. St. Catharines had 160 impressions and zero clicks. Vaughan had 80 impressions, zero clicks. Waterloo had 101 impressions, zero clicks. Since the meta titles and descriptions are set at the template level across all 24 city pages, this might be worth a quick look at the template formula — if the generated titles aren't compelling for any of these cities, they probably aren't great for most of them. A small tweak to the Webflow CMS template could lift click-through across the board.

**One data note:** the campaign-level ad spend ($107.79) doesn't match the account-level daily total ($155.43). The difference of ~$48 is likely coming from campaigns not shown in the filtered campaign list. Worth confirming all active campaigns are being pulled into the daily data so cost per new customer isn't understated.

days.analyst_brief:
**Saturday was the best placed-orders day in at least a week — and it wasn't just volume, it was broad-based.**

20 orders placed, right at the breakeven target of 20/day. Every customer segment contributed: 4 new customers (best day this week besides last Sunday's 5), 3 people coming back for their second order (the highest all week), and 13 regulars. That's not one segment carrying the others — the whole pipeline was working on Saturday.

The average order size climbed to $71.23 ex. HST, which is well above the $45 target and the $63.13 weekly average. This was flagged as an anomaly (order value more than 2x the 7d avg), but it's the good kind — bigger orders from more customers, not a single outsized order skewing things.

**Cost per new customer needs watching but is heading the right direction.** Saturday's $28.70 is above the $25 ceiling, but it's the best single-day reading this week. The 7-day average sits at $59.81, which tells you how rough the rest of the week was for acquisition efficiency. The issue is that on days with only 1 new customer (Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday), the math makes every acquisition expensive regardless of spend. Saturday's 4 new customers brought the per-customer cost down meaningfully. The fix isn't really about spend — it's about converting more of those 58 clicks into first-time orders.

**The delivered number (7) looks low but isn't a concern.** Saturday placements don't get delivered on Saturday — they flow into early-week delivery slots. The delivered revenue of $490.50 ex. HST reflects orders placed earlier in the week when volume was lower (5–10/day). Expect Monday and Tuesday delivery numbers to look strong as Saturday's 20 orders work through.

**Reorder rates are quietly strong.** The April 13 cohort — customers acquired that week — came back at a 43.8% rate within 21 days, the highest in the data set. The April 6 cohort was 33.3%, also healthy. Whatever the onboarding experience looks like right now, it's working for recent customers. The all-time repeat rate of 45.9% is above the 40% target.

**A few things in the search data worth noting:**

The term "dry cleaner near me" showed up in paid search ($2.01, 1 click, no conversion). Tumbil doesn't offer this service, so that's wasted spend. This is a pattern to flag for manual review — not recommending any keyword changes, just flagging it.

On the organic side, several city pages are getting lots of impressions but very few clicks. Hamilton: 465 impressions, 3 clicks (0.65% CTR). Ottawa: 466 impressions, 5 clicks (1.07%). Mississauga: 350 impressions, 3 clicks (0.86%). St. Catharines: 160 impressions, 0 clicks. Since the meta titles and descriptions are template-level (shared across all 24 city pages), a single improvement to the formula would lift every city page's click-through rate. These pages are ranking well enough that Google shows them — people just aren't clicking. That's a meta title/description problem, not a ranking problem, and it's probably the highest-leverage 30 minutes of work available right now.

All 42 monitored pages are indexed — no issues there.

Cancellation rate continues to trend well: the rolling 7-day rate is 1.3% (vs the 30-day baseline of 3.7%), down another 0.1 percentage points from yesterday's reading. Zero failed payments.

days.executive_summary: Sunday placed 14 orders ($771.01 ex. HST), above the 7-day average of 12.3. Deliveries surged to 15 ($800.00 ex. HST, +38.3%). But only 2 new customers were acquired at $48.91 each — nearly double the $25 ceiling. Regulars carried the day at 71% of orders.
days.analyst_brief:
**The regulars are thriving. The front door is expensive.**

Sunday's 14 placed orders and 15 deliveries are both above their 7-day averages — a good day by volume. Revenue hit $800.00 ex. HST on the delivery side, almost 40% above the weekly norm. But look at where those orders came from: 10 of the 14 placed orders (71%) were from regulars, 2 from people placing their second order, and only 2 from brand-new customers.

That matters because the cost to get those 2 new customers was $48.91 each — $97.83 in ad spend divided by 2 new sign-ups. The $25 ceiling was nearly doubled. And it's not just a Sunday blip: the 7-day rolling cost per new customer is $56.48 ($903.66 spent across the week to acquire 16 new customers). The acquisition engine is consistently running at more than 2x the target cost.

To be clear: this doesn't mean Sunday was a bad day. The business made $800 in delivered revenue on $97.83 in ad spend — that's healthy. The problem is narrower: every new customer is costing roughly twice what you'd want. The regulars are subsidizing the topline numbers, which is great for today but doesn't grow the customer base sustainably.

**One low rating to watch.** Order #4777 got a 3-star rating from WashPro #2323 (logged at 3:44 PM on May 10). It's the only sub-5-star rating in the last 7 days. No WashPro has triggered a 30-day alert pattern, so this is an isolated data point — but worth a glance at what happened with that order.

**The reorder trend is quietly encouraging.** The most recent fully-matured cohort (week of April 13) had a 41.2% reorder rate within 21 days — the best in the dataset. That's up from a trough of ~28-29% in early March. Whatever the recent customer experience is doing, it's converting first-timers into repeat customers at a better clip. This strengthens the case that the product is solid; the bottleneck is getting people through the door at a reasonable cost.

**Search observations — for awareness, not action.** Google Ads spent $1.61 on a click for "dry cleaner toronto." Tumbil doesn't offer that service, so that's pure waste. This is worth flagging for manual review of which search terms are triggering your ads — but per standing rules, no keyword changes without operator approval. Separately, "my sparkle laundry app" (a competitor name) converted a registration at $3.36 per click, which is interesting.

On the organic side, several city pages are getting seen by Google but barely clicked. Hamilton: 467 impressions, 3 clicks (0.64% CTR). Mississauga: 368 impressions, 3 clicks (0.82%). Kitchener: 306 impressions, 2 clicks (0.65%). Oakville: 124 impressions, 0 clicks. Waterloo: 117 impressions, 0 clicks. Since the meta titles and descriptions are shared across all 24 city pages via Webflow's CMS template, this is a systemic issue — tweaking the template formula could improve CTR across the board. All 42 monitored pages are indexed; no flagged pages.

**The one thing.** The cost per new customer is the persistent red flag. At $56.48 over 7 days, you're paying more than double the $25 target for every new customer. The regulars make the P&L look fine today, but the pipeline of future regulars depends on affordable acquisition. Worth a focused session on which campaigns and search terms are actually producing new customers versus burning budget on impressions that don't convert.

days.executive_summary: Monday placed 14 orders ($785.74 ex. HST, +12.8% vs 7d avg) with 14 delivered ($834.12 ex. HST, +31.8%). Regulars drove the day at 10 of 14 orders. 3 new customers acquired at $36.87 each — better than the $66.62 rolling average but still over the $25 ceiling.
days.analyst_brief:
Monday was strong — 14 placed, 14 delivered, zero cancellations. Delivered revenue hit $834.12 ex. HST, the second-best day this week. The business reliably produces $800+ revenue days when order counts reach 14-16.

**Regulars are the engine.** 10 of 14 orders came from customers on their third-or-more order (71%, right at the 7-day average of 69.5%). This is healthy — the service is sticky — but it means daily volume is capped by how many regulars happen to need laundry that day. Growth has to come from getting more new people in and converting them to repeat.

**Cost per new customer is still a problem, even on a good day.** Monday's $36.87 is the best reading this week — the 7-day average is $66.62 — but still 47% above the $25 ceiling. The math: $110.62 in ad spend, 3 new customers. To hit $25 at that spend level, you'd need 4-5 new customers daily. Week totals: $866.10 spent, 13 new customers = $66.62 each.

The issue is conversion, not traffic. Monday got 66 ad clicks from 1,422 impressions (4.6% click rate, which is fine). But 66 clicks yielded only 3 new customers — ~4.5% click-to-first-order rate. Are those clicks landing on pages that make booking easy? Are the clickers actually in serviceable areas?

days.analyst_brief:

Yesterday produced 1 new customer on $130.69 of ad spend. That's $130.69 to acquire a single person — more than 5x the $25 ceiling. The 7-day rolling cost per new customer is $69.82 (12 new customers from $837.83 in ads), which is still nearly 3x the target. This isn't a one-day blip. The good news: the people who *did* come in recently are coming back. Three second-order customers showed up Tuesday (vs the 1.7 average), which means recent first-timers are converting to repeat buyers. And the Apr 13 weekly cohort — the most recent one with 21 days of data — had 41.2% of new customers come back for a second order, the highest rate since the Mar 23 cohort (38.9%). The product is working for people who try it.

But the Apr 20 cohort is a red flag: only 1 of 10 new customers came back (10%). That's the worst reorder rate in the entire dataset. It's a small cohort so it could be noise, but it's worth watching whether the May cohorts recover or whether something changed about who's signing up.

**The placed average order size fell to $43.60 ex. HST, below the $45 floor.** That's -24.4% vs the 7-day average of $57.70. The drop is almost entirely about who ordered: 1 new customer and 3 second-order customers made up nearly a third of Tuesday's orders, and newer customers tend to place smaller first or second loads. The 9 regulars ordered at their usual clip. This isn't a pricing problem — it's a composition shift on a day where the order mix skewed toward smaller, newer accounts.

Deliveries were strong at 17 orders and $920.38 ex. HST in revenue, clearing weekend pickups. That's +49.1% over the 7-day delivery average. The delivery side of the business is healthy.

**On the ad spend side:** the GTA Click campaign consumed half the budget ($66.58) and produced 2 registrations (not orders — Google Ads counts registrations as conversions). Hamilton got 1 registration for $10.34. The search terms driving clicks are overwhelmingly "laundromat near me" and "laundry near me" — people looking for physical laundromats, not pickup-and-delivery. That's a persistent mismatch between what people search for and what Tumbil actually offers. The terms that describe what Tumbil does — "laundry pickup and delivery," "wash and fold service near me" — get far fewer impressions.

One search term to flag for manual review: "dry cleaner prices" appeared and got a click ($2.76 spent). Tumbil doesn't do dry cleaning, so that click was wasted. I'm not recommending any negative keyword changes — that requires operator approval — just noting it.

Kitchener is similar: 270 impressions, 1 click, 0.37% CTR at position 6.2. Ottawa: 341 impressions, 4 clicks, 1.17% CTR. These pages are being shown to people but nobody's clicking. The meta title and description are template-level (shared across all 24 city pages), so any improvement would apply everywhere — worth evaluating whether the current template formula is compelling enough, since several city pages have strong impression volume but weak click-through rates.

**The one thing:** the business needs more new customers at a sustainable cost. Regulars are holding the line at ~9 orders/day and recent converts are coming back, but at 1.7 new customers/day and $69.82 each, the front of the pipeline is too thin and too expensive. The ad spend is finding people who want a laundromat, not people who want pickup and delivery. That's the gap to close.

This is good news and bad news. Good: you have a loyal base that keeps ordering. Bad: the growth engine — turning strangers into customers — is sputtering.

Wednesday's cost per new customer was $50.36 — exactly double the $25 ceiling. That's actually the *best* day of the week. The 7-day average is $64.08 (total 7d ad spend of $833 ÷ 13 new customers). You're spending roughly $119/day on ads and acquiring fewer than 2 new customers per day.

If regulars are consistently ordering smaller, that's a revenue headwind even as order count grows.

Only 10 deliveries for $502.50 ex. HST — down 22.6% from the 7d average. This likely reflects lighter placement days earlier in the week working through the delivery cycle. The 7-day delivery trend has been solid (82 deliveries, $4,542 ex. HST), so this should normalize.

days.analyst_brief:

18 orders placed, zero cancelled, $1,061 in order value ex. HST — second-best day of the week after Tuesday's 19. Deliveries came in strong at 16 for $1,118 ex. HST in revenue, the highest revenue day in the 7-day window. The delivered average order size was $69.88 ex. HST, well above the $56.80 average — someone placed a big load or two.

The customer mix tells the real story. 12 of 18 orders came from regulars, 3 from people placing their second order, and 3 from brand-new customers. That second-order number is worth noticing: 3 vs a 1.9 daily average. Three people came back for their second order on a single day. Over the past 8 weeks, the share of new customers who ordered again within 21 days has averaged around 31%, with the best cohort (week of Apr 13) hitting 41%. The retention engine is working — people who try Tumbil tend to come back.

The persistent red flag is cost per new customer. Saturday: $35.05. Seven-day average: $38.86. The ceiling is $25. That means Tumbil spent $105 on ads and got 3 new customers, each costing 40% more than the target. Over the full week, $777 in ad spend produced 20 new customers at $38.86 each — 55% above the ceiling.

The hard question: are those 3 new customers even attributable to the ads? Google Ads reported zero conversions (even registrations) on Saturday across every campaign. The conversion tracking gap (it counts registrations, not purchases) makes it impossible to know from the Ads data alone which clicks turned into orders. What we do know is that $105 went out the door, and separately 3 new customers showed up. Whether the ads caused those signups or whether those people found Tumbil through organic search, word of mouth, or something else — the data can't say.

This matters because the response to "CPA is too high" depends on whether ads are producing customers inefficiently or producing almost no customers at all. If the ads are working but expensively, you'd optimize creative and targeting. If the ads are mostly generating clicks that don't convert, the spend itself might be the issue. Worth a manual look at the last 30 days: how many new customers can actually be traced back to an ad click?

**On the organic side**, a few city pages are getting seen by Google but not clicked. Kitchener: 302 impressions over the past week, 0 clicks, average position 6.4. Ottawa: 550 impressions, 3 clicks (0.55% CTR), position 11. These are template-driven pages with shared meta titles, so any meta-title change would affect all 24 cities — not a quick per-page fix. But the body content on those pages is unique per city, and improving it could help rankings and click-through. Kitchener in particular: position 6.4 means it's showing on page 1, getting seen, and getting ignored. That's a content problem, not a ranking problem.

**Ratings**: 4.92 average, 12 of 13 ratings were 5 stars, zero low ratings. The 91.6% response rate on the rating prompt continues to be excellent. One WashPro (ID 2323) had two ratings of 3 over the past week — not alarming, but if it continues, worth checking in.

days.analyst_brief:
Wednesday was the weakest placement day in the 7-day window — only 7 orders placed, against an average of 13.6. That's not a record low (Saturday the 17th also hit 11), but the combination of low volume and high ad spend makes it sting. You spent $126.04 on ads and got 2 new customers out of it, putting the cost per new customer at $63.02 — more than double the $25 ceiling and well above the 7-day rolling average of $34.48.

The delivery side tells a different story. 17 orders delivered at $1,037.27 ex. HST is the best revenue day in the past week. That's the pipeline from prior days flushing through, not a signal about Wednesday's demand.

**What's behind the 7-order day.** Looking at the week, placement volume has been lumpy: 11, 16, 18, 11, 15, 17, then 7. Wednesday didn't continue a trend — it broke one. Monday and Tuesday were actually strong (15 and 17 placed). The drop is sharp enough that it's worth asking whether something went wrong operationally (app issues, coverage gaps) or whether this is just a noisy midweek dip. Without GA4 session data, there's no way to tell whether fewer people showed up or whether the same number showed up and didn't convert.

**The acquisition math.** Two new customers on a $126 spend day is not a pattern you can sustain. But one day doesn't make a pattern — the 7-day cost per new customer is $34.48 (22 new customers on $758.57 total spend), which is still above the $25 ceiling but not alarmingly so. The real question is whether acquisition efficiency is trending the wrong way. Looking at the daily new-customer counts this week — 2, 7, 3, 2, 2, 4, 2 — only Thursday's 7 was clearly strong. The other six days averaged 2.5 new customers each. That's thin.

**Ad spend composition.** The GTA Click campaign took $64.77 (half the budget) with 44 clicks and zero tracked conversions. Google Ads conversion tracking still counts registrations, not purchases, so the 0.0 conversions number isn't alarming on its own — but it does mean you're flying blind on which campaigns actually produce orders. The city campaigns are small individually ($1–$10 each) but collectively add up. Some search terms are clearly off-target: "washer dryer combo rental" ($3.54, 2 clicks), "coin laundry near me" ($4.04, 2 clicks), "steam press near me" ($1.85) — these are people looking for physical laundromats or appliance rentals, not pickup-and-delivery. Worth flagging for manual review, but not something to act on without operator approval.

**The customer mix.** Five of Wednesday's 7 orders came from regulars. Zero second-order customers. That's concerning if it becomes a pattern — you need people coming back for a second order to build the habit loop. The week-of-Apr-27 cohort (the most recent with enough time to measure) shows a 25% reorder rate within 21 days, down from 41% for the week of Apr 13. Small sample sizes (8 vs 17 new customers) make this noisy, but it's the second week in a row below 30%. Worth watching.

**One bright spot: service quality.** Perfect 5.0 average rating yesterday across 11 reviews. The 7-day average is 4.81, and the response rate is 76% — customers are reviewing and they're happy. WashPro 2323 has three low ratings in the past 30 days (average 3.0) and is the only flagged operator. That's a conversation worth having.

**The one thing today:** Understand why Wednesday placed orders collapsed. Check whether there was an app or coverage issue. If operations were normal, then the problem is at the front of the order pipeline — not enough new people finding Tumbil, and the regulars took a day off. The 7-day cost per new customer at $34.48 is already $9.48 over the $25 ceiling; if Wednesday's pattern repeats, the economics break fast.

days.executive_summary: Sunday placed orders were soft but not alarming: 12 vs 12.9 avg (-7.0%), helped by a large $63.06 average order size ex. HST. The real issue was acquisition: $134.65 in ads produced 2 new customers, or $67.33 each vs the $25 ceiling.
days.analyst_brief:
Yesterday was not a demand-collapse day. It was a weak new-customer day hidden inside an otherwise decent Sunday.

The good news: 12 placed orders is only 7.0% below the 7-day average, and placed order value was $756.77 ex. HST, 7.0% above average. That happened because the average order size was strong at $63.06 ex. HST, safely above the $45 target and nearly 15% above the 7-day average. The day’s orders were mostly regulars: 8 of 12 came from customers on their third order or later. That is healthy proof that the service keeps working for people who have already tried it.

The problem is what paid spend bought. Google Ads spent $134.65 and the DB shows only 2 new customers. That is $67.33 per new customer, almost 2.7x the $25 ceiling. The 7-day cost per new customer is also high at $49.14, so this is not just one noisy Sunday. Do not soften this by spreading ad spend across all 12 orders: 10 of those orders were returning customers, and yesterday’s acquisition budget did not create them. The clean read is: paid ads brought in too few new customers for the money.

The delivery numbers look ugly but should be read as an operations timing signal, not a growth signal by themselves. Only 4 orders delivered, with $244.63 ex. HST revenue, both less than half the 7-day average. That deserves a check for whether Sunday had fewer scheduled deliveries or a route/supply constraint. It does not change the marketing conversion read, because placed orders are the demand number.

The highest-leverage move today is to tighten paid acquisition without touching negatives. The obvious place to inspect is the GTA Click campaign: it spent $56.22, more than 40% of yesterday’s spend, for 1 Google Ads registration, while the whole account only produced 2 DB new customers. Before changing bids or keywords, line up yesterday’s new-customer orders against campaign/city signals and ask: did GTA Click produce actual first orders, or mostly clicks and registrations? If the answer is mostly registrations, shift budget toward the city campaigns showing real order creation once that mapping is visible. Hamilton and Ottawa each reported 2 Google Ads registrations on modest spend; they are worth checking against DB first-order geography before moving money.

Organic search has a smaller but cleaner opportunity: Hamilton’s city page had 414 impressions, ranked around position 9.9, and got only 1 click. Because the city pages share template-level meta title and description, do not treat this as a one-page title tweak. The body content is the editable per-city lever. A 20-minute Hamilton body copy pass focused on pickup/delivery, wash and fold, and local intent is reasonable if paid-ad investigation is already in motion.

Service quality remains acceptable but watch WashPro 2323: 4 low ratings in 30 days at a 2.75 average is enough to merit operator review. Yesterday’s cancellation rate moved slightly down vs yesterday’s rolling reading (-0.1pp), and failed payments stayed at zero.
